sentences.text: Where are we going?
Siberia?
I was never there.
They left something there.
Well, what really happened when they-- when the Russians and the Germans divided Poland in 1939, our-- our town was divided in 1/2.
It was divided by a river, San-- S-A-N.
We lived on the Russian side for awhile.
And I, as a child, went to a Russian school.
I learned Russian.
I learned it quickly.
I was very proficient at it.
I could read books, speak, and the grammar was perfect.
But I'm sorry to say, I forgot most of it today.
[LAUGHS] One thing I remember specifically in Russian school-- in the afternoon, every child belonged to Communist Party.
And we had to go and exercise.
It was part of a child's routine to be in good physical shape.
We wore uniforms.
And they were very strict, the Russians.
There was no persecution whatsoever.
The most amazing thing was when the Russians came-- and they came from behind the Iron Curtain-- this was the first country, Western country, that they have seen.
The watches was most prized possessions.
They would buy 10, 12 watches at a time.
They have never seen running-- flush toilets.
So they didn't know what it was.
So the women would go into the toilets and pick up the water and cook with it.
The Russian soldiers-- we could not cope with.
Because a Russian soldier didn't need very much.
He would have a piece of black bread and a piece of pork lard, put it under his pillow at night-- not refrigerate it-- in the morning, pick it up, cut a piece of bread and lard.
And that was the breakfast-- or maybe lunch, too-- I mean, when you think in compared with American army.
During the war, when the Russians were there, things were quite calm, basically, until Hitler decided to move east.
And he wanted to conquer Russia and go all the way to Moscow, which he did not accomplish.
When the German soldiers came to Russia in the middle of the winter, and the Russians brought in the soldiers from Siberia-- and the Siberian soldiers were hot.
They were taking off their jackets while the Germans were freezing off their noses and their fingers.
You could not compare.
And it was not possible for Russia to win the war.
And they lost it right there.
Well, as we know, later, the Russians started pushing west.
But that's history of Europe.
At one point, yes.
We ran away from our town to a town 100 miles away, named Lvov-- L-V-O-V.
And my mother, who happen to have been a very, very capable and self-educated person, accomplished to get for us papers that we were working people-- commoners.
That meant that they didn't pick us up and didn't take us to Siberia.
The Russians used to come at night, knock at the door-- the same way like the Germans did later-- give people 10 minutes to pack, and telling them, come on, let's go.
I am not even sure they were telling them where they went.
And almost all of them went to Siberia, to the freezing land.
As a matter of fact, my brother-in-law was in Siberia.
Many people suffered tremendously, but nobody was killed.
Many died for various reasons, but they died on their own natural death, the same way like my cousin survived and came back to Poland.
His name was Artek, A-R-T-E-K.
He lives in Israel now with his wife and his family.
His name-- last name was Engelhart-- E-- [LAUGHS] E-N-G-E-L-H-A-R-T.
Today, they shorten it, and his name is Erel-- E-R-E-L.
Right, and that's how we managed to stay in the city.
And eventually, we went back to our town, because we had the papers.
But she could not get the papers in our town, because everybody knew us.
In a strange city, it was easier.
Well, nobody can predict that, because many people died.
But they always hoped that the weather and the work would not have killed them.
But it didn't-- didn't happen that way.
Yes, every school was a public school in Russia.
And I must tell you, I wish that we have here in United States as good of schools, as much attention paid to the children, as they-- we had in Russia.
The children in Russia get everything the best.
Education, supervision, no matter what it takes, the government paid for it.
But they are the kings and queens of the country-- small children.
They have everything, regardless what type of family they come from.
And I think this is very, very smart for any nation to do.
I'm-- I'm continuously fighting for it right now here in United States.
Various subjects-- Medieval History-- I remember learning Latin.
Remember, I was in seventh grade.

Now, if you want to know about education, in Poland, a large percentage of the children went through sixth grade, and if they didn't want to continue to higher education, there was a seventh grade, like a combination.
The ones that went to higher education continued.
As a result, we had, in six grades, a complete education, including geography of the world, including histories of the world, including one or two languages, plus math.
I remember all the way through geometry learning in sixth grade.
And that was an awful lot, and was a very good education.
Now, the Russian education followed that.
So we were already on a different plateau to learn.
And even so-- we were in seventh, eighth grade-- there was quite a sophisticated education.
Well, certainly, this was wartime.
And things were not readily available.
But it wasn't bad.
We had enough food.
We didn't have the selection, maybe, what we had before the war.
But there was no problem with food whatsoever.
We still had our three meals a day-- our main meal in the middle of the day-- the large one, like still large parts of the world have it today-- and a supper-- what we call it-- in the evening.
No, I do not.
They were very, very strict about hiding it from me.
They wanted to overprotect me.
I wish it wasn't that way, but that's what happened.
When the war started I didn't even know why.
I had no idea whatsoever.
And every day they were telling me.
When things started happening, they would give me a 1/2 explanation, not a complete one.
Well, they would say, the war is on, and we have to move.
I didn't know why we had to move.
I didn't even realize exactly who was the main enemy.
I went to school, and I had friends.
And I had to exercise and go to gym, and whatsoever.
That was my schedule.
The schedule was pretty normal still, under Russian-- until later, when the Germans came and started moving along.
So there was under Russians.
I think it was almost two years.
And then the Russians started going west.
They took over the other part of the city.
And I think they went all the way to the border at that point.
They also-- the Germans had an idea they want to get rid of the Jews.
For absolutely no other reason-- but they pick the Jews, the gypsies, the handicapped, the psychologically ill, as their scapegoats.
Hitler wanted to get elected in 1933, and he didn't know how to do it.
He knew that many people were afraid of the communism.
So he decided to make-- promise the people that he's going to make a clean, German-blooded race.
And anyone that had any mixed blood, even three, four generations back, was not pure blood.
That's why he started searching out everybody that was 1/2 Jewish, 1/4 Jewish, intermarried, maybe great grandparents somewhere were intermarried.
That was enough to eliminate.
But in the beginning-- I have learned that after the war-- Hitler did not want to kill Jews, or gypsies, or all these people.
He wanted to get rid of them, but no country in the world wanted to accept them.
That's why-- anybody realizes it-- for us, Israel is so, so important.
Because we feel if we had a country, we could run somewhere.
But if we did not have a country, we couldn't do anything about it.
So even we don't live there or anything like that-- but as long as we have a voice somewhere.
In case of some problems, we have a place to go to-- in case of emergency.
The Germans started to get-- oh, I think this is where we were at that point.
I remember once an incident when the SS men came along, and I found myself somewhere in a workshop.
I don't know why.
I didn't know what to do.
So I finally saw a shelf above me.
I grab few cans of paint and put it in front of me, and laid down completely still.
They came.
They looked.
They looked.
I was-- my heart was beating so loud that I was afraid that's what they're going hear.
Because I wasn't breathing.
Somehow, the death did not have me on the list.
Because this was the first encounter with death at that point.
And they did not discover me.
Many other incidents happened later.
But anyway, right now, what I want to tell you is myself being a young girl, the Germans wanted to use my strengths-- my muscles.
And they selected me to break large boulders into tiny little rocks.
They would give me a big hammer-- a mallet, you call it-- or a pick.
I had all of these tools.
And all day long I would pound these huge, big rocks with my two hands.
And I developed tremendous muscles.
I was so strong that I was ashamed of it.
And as a result of that, as I started going to different experiences through the war, I was able to do various things, because I was strong.
And maybe part of it saved me, too.
Because as you will hear later, I was continuously sent to work.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
These were people who thought that they're going to save their own life by being Jewish policemen.
So all the Germans had to do was come over and give orders.
I would like to have today 500 Jews.
You organize them.
And these men went out into the public and brought 500 people.
When the Germans were coming at night, or when these policemen were bringing the people, all people were told is that they're going to work-- no address, no location.
You have 10 minutes to pack your clothes.
And come with us, and we'll take you to work.
The fact is that they tried to camouflage what they were doing continuously.
Because there weren't many Germans.
And I do believe that they didn't know about it.
Even so, most of the population, I think, knew.
But there were many that still never realized what the Germans were doing.
They were doing other jobs, or just sitting in the place.
There was not too many jobs around.
There was just idleness-- sit around and do nothing.
Oh, we did have to wear yellow armbands with the Star of David in the ghetto.
I do want to mention at this point that there was one country in this world-- and one and only-- a small little country-- that was Denmark.
When Hitler came to the Danish King, and he says, 'Tell us who your Jews are,' the king put on the yellow armband on the horse.
He told everybody in the country to put it on.
And all of the sudden, he wanted to show the German that they were all Jews.
Well, they couldn't kill the whole population.
They knew it wasn't true.
And that's how the Jews got saved.
That was the only country that came out with this kind of an idea, and it was most benevolent.
I mean, it was absolutely terrific.
Yes, they were moved out-- non-Jewish neighbors.
And all of a sudden, nobody knew us.
All our friends, non-Jewish friends, suddenly weren't our friends.
If one hears about the righteous gentiles, it's correct.
But it was a very small percentage.
And I would say majority of it was done for money.
There were very few people that were idealistic or religious enough.
There were some.
There was a whole city in-- in France-- Sherbrooke-- where the whole population in the little city decided to hide out all the Jews for no other reason but this is-- we are our brothers keepers.
And it's not right to have decent human beings killed.
And that was one of these very wonderful things that did happen.
And there were many more, but comparatively, a very small percentage.
Oh, sure-- a lot of it.
My husband used to know a lot of those people.
In Poland, the situation was very bad.
I don't know why.
And the only way I can explain it is that the-- in Polish church, there were many people that were very, very anti-Semitic.
Because even before the war, before Hitler started the war, there were many Polish individuals in the church that would go to Hitler and teach them about anti-Semitism, and tell them what do.
There are whole books now written-- what happened in Poland before the war when nobody knew about it.
Nobody heard about it.
I certainly didn't know about that.
But it did exist.
I am hoping, as far as that goes-- anti-Semitism-- and right now, finally, the first step was made.
Anti-Semitism is a disease that people have that they don't really know why-- people that have never met any Jews.
They think Jews have horns on the head.
They are some kind of outcasts-- until they meet some people.
And it comes, a lot of it, from the Catholic catechism, where the children are taught in the very young age that Jews killed Jesus Christ.

And they're not told what really happened, that the Romans killed Jesus Christ-- and this was not the case-- and that Jesus Christ really was a Jew for quite a long time until Peter came over and started changing things around.
Oh, it was shattering-- absolutely.
I mean, my friends that I was playing with-- girls I played with dolls, and danced ballet, or whatever, suddenly, they didn't know me.
They forgot me.
They were told by their parents not to play with me, that I was different.
I was not a full human being.
I was, you know, a slave, or whatever-- pyorrhea, cancer-- [LAUGHS] whatever it was.
And it was bad.
The ghetto was an area that was fenced in with electric wires.
We lived, the whole family, in one room.
As a matter of fact, there was some other people in the same room.
We slept on the floor.
Thanks to my mother's foresight, I was-- I had no problem sleeping on a straw sack, because we had no mattresses.
And we had no sleeping bags.
But the floor was hard and rough, and I had no problem.
My parents had no problem sleeping on a straw sack either.
Straw sack-- it's a lot of straw and a piece of material.
But sometimes it comes out and sticks you.
Food was-- there wasn't sufficient amount.
[LAUGHS] There was very little food.
We couldn't go to the store and buy food.
You have to barter it, swap, organize.
The farmers would be coming to our little town.
It wasn't too far.
And they would bring a sack of potatoes, and we would give them, maybe, some valuables for it.
And then we shared it with some other people.
And that's how the food-- we managed.
Very little meat-- I mean, meat was unheard of whatsoever.
I mean, potatoes, and vegetables, and maybe some fruit in the season was available.
Now, I do want to bring up that there was a tremendous traffic in the sewer.
My husband was one of them.
He belonged to a underground, and their job was to buy ammunition on the outside-- not food.
There was another group that dealt with food-- [LAUGHS] and bring it into the ghetto for protection.
To tell you how bad it was-- my husband, who was in the army when the war broke out-- and he was one of the first few being caught into one of the camps.
He went into Majdenek-- M-A-J-D-E-N-E-K.
He did accomplish, because it was in the beginning of the war, to get some papers from the governor-- and with bribing.
Because he had a lot of gold and diamonds, because his job in the camp was to unpack the suitcases that the people brought before they went in to being killed.
I'm not sure there was a gas chamber yet.
He had an awful lot of gold and diamonds.
And he didn't know what to do with it.
So he managed to bribe a Ukrainian guard and a Latvian guard-- and they were the worst.
And his whole bribe was that they deliver a letter to a governor-- well, first, to his girlfriend, and then a governor in turn.
And he got an official permission to leave.
Because he was working for the governor.
This was the beginning of the war.
And he came home to his own parents when the whole family was intact, and told them that times are-- bad times are coming.
Leave the place-- run.
Nobody believed him.
Don't get attached to possessions.
Save your life-- is my motto for everyone-- regardless who.
Because once there were Jews persecuted.
Tomorrow, there will be any other religion.
We don't know what United States or the world holds.
My prediction is-- now, this has nothing to do with Holocaust-- that eventually, China will rule the world.
This is a very, very harsh thing to say, But I don't know why I think so, but to me, I see it very clearly.
That's a future prediction of mine.
Yes.
Well, the ghetto was getting smaller and smaller.
Because at night, they would pick up people continuously, put them on the truck, and take them, quote, unquote, 'to work'.
And evidently, my parents felt that the time is coming that they have to do something about it.
And they, with all the people in the apartment house, decided to dig a bunker.
Bunker is a hole in the wall.
People that are in the army know what bunker is.
And they started digging at night and bring the soil out.
And we had to-- we, the women-- had to dry it and throw it out the following day.
Because wet soil would be suspicious if anybody found it in the trash.
After awhile-- I don't recall how long it took-- the hole, about 20 feet by 30 feet, was dug.
Certain area was a little bit higher, and this was the area where you could sit.
In the corner there was a deeper hole, and that served as a bathroom.
The entrance was camouflaged by a couple boards on hinges.
It's like today we have in a den-- plywood.
And a couple of the boards could be picked up.
And this was the door.
And behind there, there would be steps to go down into that hole.
When my parents felt it was getting more and more dangerous, 30 people went into that hole.
There were old people coughing, and sneezing, and making noise, and little babies crying-- some all different ages.
And there was not enough room for everybody to lay down.
So if one person lay, the other one had to stand up.
Every day somebody would go out, sneak out during daytime when there was a lot of noise, and organize some food.
One day, they couldn't find anything but beans, dried beans.
And you know how long beans have to cook.
And we had no light.
We had no electricity.
We had no stove.
We had nothing!
We proceeded cooking the beans on a candle heat.
[LAUGHS] And I was the one that had to hold the candle.
I was holding it with the right hand, and my finger got all infected.
And until today, I have a scar.
Obviously, we had no Band-Aids.
We had no medication.
And that's why I have a scar.
Otherwise, it probably would have healed much nicer.
[LAUGHS]
I had to cook--
